title: Citation Guide
text:
This website uses a centralized bibliography system. All references are stored in references.bib and can be cited in any .qmd file.


Add new entries to references.bib in BibTeX format. Common entry types:


@article{authorYear,
  title={Article Title},
  author={Last, First and Last, First},
  journal={Journal Name},
  volume={10},
  number={2},
  pages={123--145},
  year={2024}
}



@book{authorYear,
  title={Book Title},
  author={Last, First},
  year={2023},
  publisher={Publisher Name}
}




Use the @ symbol followed by the citation key:

In-text citation: @authorYear renders as: Author (Year)
Parenthetical: [@authorYear] renders as: (Author, Year)
Multiple citations: [@author2024; @other2023]
With page numbers: [@authorYear, pp. 42-43]
Suppress author: [-@authorYear] renders as: (Year)




According to @example2024, this is important. 
Other research supports this claim [@examplebook2023; @exampleconf2025].



The website uses APA style by default (configured in _quarto.yml). You can change this by:

Download a different .csl file from the Zotero Style Repository
Save it in your project directory
Update the csl: field in _quarto.yml

Common styles: apa.csl, chicago.csl, ieee.csl, nature.csl



All cited references automatically appear on the References page.

title: Coding Practice
text:
Now that you have a sense of the purpose and methodology behind matching methods, we can attempt a simple application of coding this in practice. You are welcome to use your coding language of preference (Stata, R, or Python). But I will presume that you have both established your working environment to execute the code and familiarity in the language to adequately troubleshoot your technical issues.
For this exercise, we will be using data from XXX. You can download the sample data set here .
